name: Souletin Basque (Zuberera)

ancestors.notes: Souletin inherits the core Basque grapheme inventory from Batua but adds the unique grapheme ⟨ü⟩ for /y/ and has extensive phonological divergence due to French contact.

notes: Souletin (Zuberera/Xiberotarra). Easternmost Basque dialect, spoken in Soule/Zuberoa (Pyrénées-Atlantiques, France); the most phonologically divergent variety, in close contact with Gascon Occitan. VALIDATED and EXTENDED against Hualde (2018) 'Aspiration in Basque' and Egurtzegi (2013) 'Phonetics and Phonology'. Distinctive features: (1) FRONT ROUNDED VOWEL /y/ (written ü), the only Basque dialect to have developed a sixth vowel; it arose from an older /u/ by near-context-free fronting and is realised between French /y/ and /ø/ (Egurtzegi 2013: 127-128, citing Michelena FHV:52 and Hualde 1993). (2) THREE-WAY PLOSIVE CONTRAST: unlike all western varieties, Souletin contrasts voiced /b d ɡ/, voiceless unaspirated /p t k/ and voiceless ASPIRATED /pʰ tʰ kʰ/ as full phonemes (Hualde 2018: 2, citing Lafon 1958/1999). Modelled here as conditioned variants of /p t k/ because standard orthography does not mark the aspiration distinction (engine limit). (3) TWO ASPIRATE PHONEMES: an oral laryngeal /h/ and a nasalised laryngeal /h̃/ (Hualde 2018: 2, citing Hualde 1993). (4) CONTRASTIVE NASALISED VOWELS: Souletin is the only living dialect that has kept the old Basque nasalised-vowel series, appearing around a nasal consonant (khatiña [khatĩã], gazna [gaznã], ama [ãmã]) and in stressed word-final position after apocope of an old /-n/ (ardu [arðũ] < *ardano 'wine'), where they contrast with plain final vowels (Egurtzegi 2013: 127); Souletin lacks nasal /õ/, which became /ũ/ after /y/ arose (Egurtzegi 2013: 127). NOTE (engine limit): vowel nasality and the aspirated/unaspirated stop split are NOT recoverable from standard Basque orthography, so they are documented here but not emitted at the grapheme→IPA level; only the ü→/y/ mapping and the aspirated-stop variants are modelled. Endangered, with a few thousand active speakers.

sources.author: Hualde, J.I.
sources.title: Aspiration in Basque
sources.notes: Primary read source: Souletin three-way plosive contrast and the oral /h/ + nasal /h̃/ aspirate phonemes (p.2).

sources.author: Egurtzegi, A.
sources.title: Phonetics and Phonology (ch. 4). In M. Martínez-Areta (ed.), Basque and Proto-Basque
sources.notes: Primary read source: Souletin front rounded /y/ (pp.127-128) and the contrastive nasalised-vowel series shared with Roncalese (p.127).

sources.author: Hualde, J.I. & Ortiz de Urbina, J. (eds.)
sources.title: A Grammar of Basque

sources.author: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I.
sources.title: The Sounds of the World's Languages

location.notes: Zuberoa (Soule) — the historical province the dialect is named for. [Wikidata Q673040 P625]
